twitter_thread: 72% of user surveys I've reviewed have response rates below 5%. Is that really enough to gauge 'Happy'?
App retention rates, like 60-day, are a better metric. Or time-to-value, such as 3-day onboarding.
Surveys with 10+ questions lose 40% of users. Limit to 3-5 and use tools like SurveyMonkey or Typeform.
25% of users drop off when faced with open-ended questions. Use multiple choice or rating scales instead.
A/B testing with Optimizely or VWO can validate 'Happy' metrics. Try testing 2-3 variables at a time.
Segmenting users by behavior, like power users vs casual, can reveal differing 'Happy' metrics.
What's the most effective way you've measured 'Happy' in your software projects? #softwaredevelopment #userexperience
pack.linkedin: Measuring 'Happy' in software development often relies on user surveys, but 72% of these surveys have response rates below 5%. Instead, consider using concrete metrics like 60-day app retention rates or 3-day onboarding time-to-value. Tools like SurveyMonkey or Typeform can help with survey creation, while Optimizely or VWO can assist with A/B testing. Segmenting your user base, such as by behavior, is also crucial. What methods have you found to be most effective in measuring user satisfaction in your software projects?
tiktok_hooks: Your user surveys are probably useless
Why survey questions are killing your response rate
pack.reddit_headline: Measuring 'Happy' in software: are surveys a waste of time?
